worth_it_explanation: The PubMatic seat and account structure is the spine that reporting, payments, and supply-chain identity hang from, so a fragmented or stale structure splits the numbers a publisher reconciles and the seller record buyers read. The audit is a review of accounts and seats the publisher already has, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete consolidation or close-out the publisher can act on. Confirming the structure once tells the publisher whether their revenue rolls up cleanly and whether every active seat still belongs in the supply chain.
intro: A publisher monetizing through PubMatic sits inside an account and seat structure that decides how inventory is grouped, how revenue rolls up for reporting and payment, and how the publisher is represented to buyers as a seller. Over time that structure can fragment as new properties, agency relationships, or test setups add accounts and seats that were never consolidated or closed. These checks confirm that the account and seat structure is deliberate and current, so reporting and payments roll up to one clean place and the seller record buyers read matches the entities the publisher actually runs.

audit_checks.title: Inventory is split across fragmented or duplicate seats
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the same inventory is split across more than one seat or account, reporting fragments and payments route to more than one place, so a publisher reconciles several partial pictures instead of one. The split also weakens the seller record because the same property can surface under more than one seller_id in sellers.json, which makes the publisher's supply path harder for a buyer to verify.

educate.base:
PubMatic identifies a selling entity by a seat and the account it belongs to, and that identity is what carries through reporting, payment, and the public seller record. When a publisher's inventory is grouped under a single, intended seat structure, revenue rolls up to one place, payments route to one entity, and buyers reading the supply chain see one clean seller. When the same inventory is spread across more than one seat or duplicate account, each of those threads splits.

Fragmentation usually accumulates rather than being chosen. A new property gets its own account that was never folded into the main one. An agency or a managed relationship sets up a parallel seat. A test or onboarding seat is created and left active after it stops being used. Each addition is reasonable on its own, and none of them announce that the structure has drifted, so the split only shows up when someone tries to reconcile the whole picture.

The cost of a fragmented structure shows up in three places:
  • Reporting: revenue and performance for one property appear under more than one seat, so no single report shows the property whole and the publisher reconciles partial views
  • Payments: separate seats can route to separate payment entities or terms, which complicates reconciliation and can delay or misallocate revenue
  • Seller identity: the same property can surface under more than one seller_id in the publisher's sellers.json record, which makes the supply path harder for a buyer to verify against the ads.txt entries on the publisher's own pages
The audit question is whether the seats and accounts in place reflect a deliberate structure or whether the same inventory has fragmented across duplicates that should roll up to one. You may want to map every active seat and account to the property and the business relationship it represents, then confirm that no single property is split across seats that could be consolidated. One approach is to start from the publisher's own list of properties and work outward to the seats, rather than starting from the seat list and assuming each one is meant to exist.

site_type_overlays.publishing: A media publisher running several titles or sections can end up with a separate seat per title that grew up independently, even though they reconcile and get paid as one business. You may want to confirm whether those per-title seats are a deliberate structure or fragments that should roll up to a single account, so one report shows the network whole and one seller_id represents it to buyers.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A commerce-content publisher that added a media or content arm later can end up with the new arm on its own seat, separate from the original property. You may want to confirm that the two seats are intended to stay separate for a real reason, such as distinct legal entities, rather than a duplicate that splits reporting and payment for what is operationally one business.

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's account and seat configuration documentation lives in its partner portal (login required), so the genuine public references here are the IAB Tech Lab supply-chain standards that the seat structure has to stay consistent with.

investigate.intro: Map every active PubMatic seat and account to the property and business relationship it represents, then check whether any single property is split across more than one seat that could roll up to one.

pubmatic_ui.label: PubMatic publisher console
steps: List every account and seat the publisher can access, and record the property or inventory each one carries.
For each property the publisher operates, note how many seats or accounts its inventory appears under.

Flag any property whose inventory is split across more than one seat, and record whether the split reflects a real distinction, for example a separate legal entity, or a duplicate that could consolidate.
Ask the publisher to confirm, for each active seat, the business reason it exists, and record any seat no one can account for.

sellers_json_check.label: Public sellers.json and ads.txt review
steps: Open PubMatic's sellers.json file and find every seller_id that maps to the publisher's domains.
Record whether one property surfaces under more than one seller_id, which points to a fragmented seat structure.
Compare those seller_id entries against the PubMatic lines in the publisher's own ads.txt file, and note any seat that appears in one place but not the other.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records every active seat and account alongside the property and business relationship it represents, and whether any single property is split across more than one seat. A structure where each property maps to one intended seat, or to multiple seats for a stated reason such as separate legal entities, passes. A structure where the same property is split across duplicate seats with no distinction behind the split, or where one property surfaces under more than one seller_id in sellers.json, is a fail recorded with the property and the seats it fragments across.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active seat and account against the property it represents, and whether any property is split across seats, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has inventory split across more than one PubMatic seat for what is operationally a single property, so reporting and payments fragment across seats that could roll up to one. A fragmented seat structure splits revenue across partial reports, can route payment to separate entities or terms, and can surface the same property under more than one seller_id in PubMatic's sellers.json, which makes the supply path harder for a buyer to verify. {{voice_recommend}} mapping each property to its seats, consolidating any property that is split without a real distinction behind the split, and confirming the result lines up with the publisher's ads.txt and sellers.json entries. After the consolidation, recheck that each property rolls up to one seat and one seller record.

audit_checks.title: Inactive or orphaned seats are left open in the structure
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A seat that stops being used but stays open keeps a path into the publisher's supply chain that no one is watching, and it stays listed as an authorized seller in sellers.json and ads.txt. An unwatched, still-authorized seat is an opening for spoofed or resold inventory to claim the publisher's identity, and it muddies the seller record buyers read to verify the supply path.

educate.base:
Seats and accounts get created for reasons that expire. A property is sold or sunset, an agency relationship ends, a test seat is spun up during onboarding, or a managed setup is replaced by a direct one. The inventory stops flowing, but the seat itself often stays open because closing it is a deliberate step that nobody is prompted to take once the traffic goes quiet.

An orphaned seat is not neutral while it sits idle. As long as it stays open and authorized, it remains a live path into the publisher's supply chain. The publisher's ads.txt file may still list the seat's PubMatic line as an authorized seller, and PubMatic's sellers.json record may still carry its seller_id. Those two files are what a buyer reads to confirm that inventory claiming to be the publisher's really is, so an authorization left open for a seat no one monitors widens the surface where spoofed or resold inventory can claim the publisher's name without anyone noticing.

A few signs point to an inactive or orphaned seat:
  • A seat or account shows no delivery or revenue across a recent reporting window, with no plan to reactivate it
  • A seat maps to a property the publisher no longer operates, or to a partner relationship that has ended
  • The publisher's ads.txt still lists a PubMatic line, or sellers.json still carries a seller_id, for inventory that no longer runs
The audit question is whether every active and authorized seat still corresponds to live inventory the publisher monitors. You may want to identify any seat with no recent delivery and no reason to stay open, then close it and remove its matching ads.txt line so the seller record reflects only the seats that are genuinely in use. Closing a stale seat narrows the supply chain to entities the publisher actually watches.

site_type_overlays.publishing: A media publisher that has launched and retired titles over the years can accumulate seats tied to properties that no longer exist. You may want to cross-check the active seat list against the properties the publisher actually runs today, and close any seat whose property was sunset, along with the matching ads.txt line on any domain that still carries it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A commerce-content publisher that tested a managed monetization setup before moving to a direct one can leave the old managed seat open after the switch. You may want to confirm whether any seat tied to a former managed or agency relationship is still authorized, and close it if the relationship has ended so it cannot keep claiming the publisher's inventory.

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's steps for deactivating a seat or account are in its partner portal (login required), so the genuine public references here are the IAB Tech Lab authorized-seller standards that an open seat keeps the publisher listed in.

investigate.intro: Identify any PubMatic seat or account with no recent delivery and no reason to stay open, and check whether its authorization still lingers in the publisher's ads.txt and PubMatic's sellers.json.

pubmatic_ui.label: PubMatic publisher console
steps: List every seat and account, and pull delivery and revenue for each across a recent window such as the last 90 days.
Flag any seat with no delivery and no revenue, and record the property or relationship it was created for.
Ask the publisher whether each flagged seat has a reason to stay open, for example a paused property due to relaunch, or whether it can be closed.
Record the seats confirmed as orphaned, with the property they map to and the date delivery stopped.

sellers_json_check.label: Public sellers.json and ads.txt review
steps: Open PubMatic's sellers.json and the publisher's own ads.txt, and list the PubMatic seller_id and ads.txt lines for the publisher's domains.
Match each authorized seat against the active inventory found in the console.
Record any seller_id or ads.txt line that maps to a seat no longer delivering, which is an authorization that should be removed when the seat is closed.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each seat with its recent delivery and the property or relationship it represents, and whether its authorization still appears in ads.txt and sellers.json. A structure where every active and authorized seat maps to live inventory the publisher monitors passes. A seat with no recent delivery, no reason to stay open, and an authorization still lingering in ads.txt or sellers.json is a fail recorded with the seat, the property it mapped to, and the authorization left in place.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each seat with its recent delivery and whether its authorization still appears in ads.txt and sellers.json above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has one or more PubMatic seats with no recent delivery left open in the structure, and their authorization still appears in ads.txt or PubMatic's sellers.json. An orphaned but still-authorized seat keeps a live, unwatched path into the supply chain, which widens the surface where spoofed or resold inventory can claim the publisher's identity, and it muddies the seller record buyers read to verify the supply path. {{voice_recommend}} confirming which seats have stopped delivering with no reason to stay open, closing them, and removing their matching ads.txt lines so the seller record carries only seats genuinely in use. After the close-out, recheck that ads.txt and sellers.json list only the seats that still run live inventory.
